brief_summary: Cross-section diptych of ABS plastic. Left panel shows sparse, colorless bromine flame-retardant compounds in unyellowed plastic; a coral forward arrow labeled 'decades of UV + O2' leads to a coral-filled middle panel showing the same compounds oxidized to an amber tint. A dashed coral reverse arrow along the bottom, labeled 'H2O2 + UV', shows the oxidized compounds being chemically reduced back toward colorless — illustrating that retrobrighting reverses the same reaction rather than coating over it.
data_sources: retrobrighting-keycaps.mdx § 'What's actually turning yellow' — 'brominated flame retardants ... Over years of exposure to UV light and ambient oxygen, the bromine compounds oxidize. The oxidation byproducts are what carry the yellow-to-amber tint.'

retrobrighting-keycaps.mdx § 'What retrobrighting actually does' — 'the UV catalyzes a reaction that chemically reduces the oxidized bromine compounds back toward their original, colorless state ... Retrobrighting is chemically altering the same compound that caused the problem'
